unitTitle: KS2 Y3 Desktop publishing (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Words and pictures
lessons.objectives:
I can explain the difference between text and images
I can say that text and images send a message
I can give a good point about using text and about using images
I can use emojis kindly online
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: text · images · communicate · advantages · disadvantages. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the signs image on the board. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — text vs image. On the starter worksheet pupils choose whether DANGER (word) and a skull (picture) are text or an image, then tick why a picture sign is useful. Signs image shown.
   • Support: choose text or image for one item.  • Core: tick the true reasons.  • Challenge: name a sign that uses both.
2. (5–15) I-do → we-do — text = words you read; an image = a picture; both can send a message. Show the signs grid: a no-entry symbol needs no words; the DOUANE/ZOLL sign has words but is still hard if you cannot read the language — so text is not always clearer.
3. (15–35) You-do — on the activity worksheet, SORT items into text / image, then SORT good points into ‘good about text’ / ‘good about images’. Likely error: thinking text is always clearer → fix-words “a picture can be quicker and cross languages.” TA: prompt, do not sort it for them.
4. (35–45) Emojis — an emoji is a small image; use it to be kind, not unkind (Challenge). Screenshot the sign/poster discussed.
5. (45–50) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil shows one picture-only sign.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Safety and road signs (Teach Computing).png

lessons.title: Can you edit it?
lessons.objectives:
I can change font size, colour, and style for a purpose
I can edit text on a page
I can explain that changing text makes the message clearer
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: font · font style · communicate · template. TA: the invitation template open and ready. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — what is desktop publishing? On the starter worksheet pupils choose that it puts text and images on a page to send a message, then tick what you can change about a font (size/colour/style). Invitation image shown.
   • Support: choose one true thing about a font.  • Core/Challenge follow in the activity.
2. (5–15) I-do — desktop publishing = page layout software; a ready-made page is a template. Show the invitation; the date and time matter most, so make them stand out.
3. (15–25) We-do — the three font changes: size, colour, style. Model changing one word each way. Likely error: changing the page background instead of the text → fix-words “click the words first.”
4. (25–40) You-do — on the activity worksheet SORT changes into size/colour/style, recall the Shift and Backspace keys, then edit the invitation (bigger, brighter title). Screenshot it. TA: tell them the menu, not the button.
5. (40–50) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil shows one word they made bigger.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Great template!
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what page orientation means
I can find placeholders and say why they help
I can make a template for a magazine cover
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: portrait · landscape · orientation · placeholder · template · layout. TA: a sheet of A4 per pupil; the software open. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — a template is a page already laid out with spaces ready; choose why it helps, and that a magazine cover is portrait. (starter worksheet)
2. (5–15) I-do → we-do — orientation: portrait = tall, landscape = wide. Play “show me” turning the A4. Show a real magazine cover (big title, main picture, short text); editorial designers decide where things go.
3. (15–25) Placeholders — a placeholder is a box that holds content. Show the cover with title/picture/story/quote boxes.
4. (25–40) You-do — on the activity worksheet SORT documents into portrait/landscape, then LABEL the magazine cover’s placeholders (title, picture, story, quote). Then build their own portrait template with three placeholders. Likely error: one box used for everything → fix-words “one box for each thing.” Screenshot the template. TA: prompt, do not build it.
5. (40–50) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil shows a placeholder. Save the template for Lesson 4.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Becoming a designer
lessons.objectives:
I can choose the best place for my content
I can paste text and add pictures to my cover
I can change content after I have added it
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: desktop publishing · copy · paste. TA: last week’s templates ready to open. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — why a saved template helps (no blank page, saves time); copy then paste to bring text in. (starter worksheet)
2. (5–10) Find last week’s work — open the software, open the saved template. Likely error: starting a new blank page → fix-words “open the one you already made.”
3. (10–30) I-do → we-do — add your text: click a placeholder, type or copy-and-paste. Then add a picture from the search (follow school image-search rules). On the activity worksheet pupils put the ‘add content’ steps in ORDER, then build their cover.
4. (30–42) Make changes — move things that overlap, resize a picture, delete what you do not want. Challenge: a picture covers the title — name two fixes (move it / make it smaller). Screenshot the cover. TA: prompt, do not redesign it.
5. (42–50) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil shows one thing added and one thing changed. Save the cover.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Finished magazine cover example (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Lay it out
lessons.objectives:
I can name different layouts
I can match a layout to its purpose
I can choose a suitable layout for a job
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: layout · purpose. TA: whiteboards ready; the layouts image on the board. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — the layout is how text and pictures are set out; you can tell a page without reading it. On the starter worksheet pupils name a newspaper (headline + columns) and say a poster has a few big words and a picture.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — show a newspaper (lots of detail, columns, portrait) and a poster (few big words, one picture, eye-catching). Layout matches purpose.
3. (20–38) You-do — on the activity worksheet pupils MATCH each layout (poster/newspaper/postcard/magazine) to its purpose, SORT layouts into ‘lots of text’ / ‘a few big words’, then choose a layout for a scenario. Likely error: choosing by how it looks, not its purpose → fix-words “what job must it do?” TA: accept any layout with a good reason.
4. (38–45) Screenshot the chosen layout.
5. (45–50) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil names the layout for a quick message.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Why desktop publishing?
lessons.objectives:
I can name real-world uses of desktop publishing
I can say why desktop publishing is helpful
I can compare desktop publishing to work made by hand
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: desktop publishing · benefits. TA: whiteboards and A4 paper ready. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — what is desktop publishing? (text + images with page layout software). On the starter worksheet pupils choose the definition and tick which things are made with it. Magazines image shown.
2. (8–18) I-do → we-do — who uses it: estate agents (brochures), card companies (cards), newspaper editors (the news). On the activity worksheet pupils SORT each job to what they make.
3. (18–30) The benefits — quick to copy many times, easy to change, always neat. Support: tick the true benefits.
4. (30–42) By hand or by computer? — the time changes (easy to fix on a computer); 20 copies needed (quick to print). Core/Challenge: explain why the computer is better. Screenshot one piece of work from the unit. TA: prompt the reason, do not give it.
5. (42–50) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil gives one real benefit of desktop publishing.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
